does: This paper presents AdvGRPO, a reinforcement learning framework that enables simultaneous training of adversarial attack and defense models for language models through alternating optimization. The core innovation addresses instability in prior GRPO applications by introducing dense multi-channel rewards that capture attack effectiveness from multiple perspectives and decoupled advantage normalization to stabilize gradient updates. The training pipeline follows a curriculum strategy, beginning with single-turn attacks, progressing to closed-loop multi-turn interactions, and then bootstrapping co-training where attacker and defender models alternate updates. The results demonstrate that this approach discovers highly effective attacks that transfer across different language models and produces defenders that achieve state-of-the-art performance on standard safety benchmarks, significantly outperforming models trained with conventional safety techniques.
connects: This work builds directly on recent co-training paradigms like those using PPO and DPO for simultaneous attacker-defender optimization, responding to documented instability issues with GRPO in this setting. It connects to the broader red teaming literature that uses reinforcement learning to discover adversarial examples against large language models, extending methods that treat attack discovery as an RL optimization problem. The paper also engages with curriculum learning approaches that scaffold training from simpler to more complex attack scenarios, and connects to recent advances in reward design and training stability in RL, particularly techniques for normalizing advantages across multiple reward signals. The work intersects with safety research on adversarial robustness and the evaluation of language model defenses against adaptive threats.
enables: This framework enables more efficient discovery of novel adversarial attacks that can guide safety improvements in large language models, potentially uncovering failure modes that static evaluation methods miss. The transferability of generated attacks opens possibilities for cross-model safety improvements and adversarial training at scale. The stable GRPO formulation with dense reward signals could extend beyond red teaming to other adversarial and multi-agent RL settings where training instability has been problematic. Future work might leverage the curriculum approach to tackle increasingly sophisticated attack scenarios, automate safety evaluation by coupling attack discovery with defense validation, or apply similar co-training dynamics to other domains requiring adaptive adversarial robustness. The method also suggests pathways for developing language models with inherent resilience to novel attack types through iterative co-adaptation.
